subject: AP Computer Science Principles
theme: Algorithms and programming use sequencing, selection, and iteration to automate solutions — and procedural abstraction manages complexity by hiding details behind named procedures.
core_concepts: All algorithms are built from three building blocks: sequencing (executing steps in order), selection (IF/ELSE branching), and iteration (REPEAT and FOR EACH loops).
Lists are 1-indexed in College Board pseudocode. Access elements with list[i]; manipulate with APPEND, INSERT, REMOVE, LENGTH.
Procedures allow programmers to name and reuse blocks of code (procedural abstraction), reducing repetition and managing complexity — this is modularity.
Binary search requires a sorted list and runs in far fewer steps than linear search — but cannot be used on unsorted data.
Simulations model real-world processes to study systems that are too costly, dangerous, or impossible to test directly — they contain simplifications and assumptions.
An algorithm runs in reasonable time if its number of steps is polynomial in input size (n, n², n³). Exponential algorithms become impractical for large inputs.
The halting problem is undecidable — no algorithm can always determine whether an arbitrary program will stop or run forever. Some problems are fundamentally unsolvable.

key_terms.definition: A named location that stores a value which can change during execution.
The operation that stores a value into a variable, written as a ← expression in CB pseudocode.
A data type that holds only TRUE or FALSE; used in conditions and logical expressions.
An ordered collection of items stored under one name; elements accessed by 1-based index in CB pseudocode.
A named, reusable block of code that can accept parameters and optionally return a value.
A variable in a procedure definition that receives a value when the procedure is called.
The value sent back by a procedure to the code that called it, via the RETURN statement.
Using named procedures to hide implementation details and reduce code complexity.
A search algorithm that checks each element one by one — works on unsorted lists but is slower.
A search algorithm that halves the search area each step — requires a sorted list but is much faster.
A program that models a real-world system to study behavior without the cost or risk of real-world testing.
An approach that finds a good-enough solution in reasonable time when a perfect solution is impractical.
A problem for which no algorithm can be written that always gives a correct yes/no answer.
The classic undecidable problem: determining if any arbitrary program will eventually stop running.
Returns the remainder after dividing two integers (e.g., 10 MOD 3 = 1).
Returns a random integer between a and b, inclusive.

exam_tip: The AP exam heavily tests code tracing — be able to manually step through pseudocode with IF/ELSE, REPEAT UNTIL, and FOR EACH loops to determine exact output; also know that binary search requires a sorted list and list indices start at 1.
